segments.s289-g105: مگر here introduces a rhetorical question ('could it be that / could he') rather than the literal 'except'. زنده is an adjective 'alive' placed before the pronoun او (object), and را is the definite/object marker attached after او. بر + شهریار is a dative/goal phrase 'to the king' with بر used in the sense 'toward/to'.
segments.s289-g106: تواند is the 3rd-person form expressing ability ('can / is able'), here governing the infinitive رسانیدن 'to bring/deliver'. از کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'from the battlefield' with از = 'from' and کارزار = 'battlefield/battle'. The infinitive construction gives a potential sense: 'be able to bring'.
segments.s289-g107: همی is a continuous/adverbial particle meaning 'kept on' or 'was (still)'. راند is a past/action verb 'rode/urged (on)' with the following name بیژن as its object or patient ('he urged Bijan on'). پر از ... is a postnominal phrase 'full of' (پر + از + noun) describing Bijan's condition 'full of pain and sorrow'.
segments.s289-g108: روانش is روان ('soul, spirit') with the 3rd-person possessive suffix -ش 'his'. پر از again = 'full of', and انده here functions as the noun 'sorrow' (a poetic/archaic form). گستهم is a first-person verbal form ('I wandered' / 'I was distraught') forming a sudden personal comment by the speaker; grammatically it shifts to 1st person to report the speaker's reaction.
segments.s289-g65: بگفت is an archaic/perfect past form meaning “he said” (ب‑ prefix + گفت). این و links to the foregoing clause (“this, and…”). سستی is a noun “weakness”; گرفتش is گرفت (past of گرفتن) with the enclitic ـش = “him,” so literally “weakness seized him.” روان here is a noun “spirit/soul,” the thing afflicted by the weakness (poetic apposition: the weakness seizes his soul).
segments.s289-g66: همی بود is a literary imperfective/past-continuous sense (“was/was then”), with همی adding durative/continuous shading. بیژن is the subject placed after the verb (typical literary word order). به سر بر نوان is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic phrase: به سر = “on the head” and بر نوان is an old-form construction meaning inability/capacity (here: he could not keep/raise it). Note نوان is an archaic form (not modern توان) and does not match the provided sense list.
segments.s289-g67: وز here is a literary/prepositional particle meaning “from” (poetic variant of از); آن جایگه = “that place.” اسب او is the direct object (“his horse”) with the possessive pronoun او after the noun (common in older Persian). بیدرنگ is an adverb “immediately,” placed after the object for emphasis.
segments.s289-g68: بیاورد is past tense “he brought” (third-person). و coordinates the following verb. بگشاد is a literary past (ب‑ prefix + گشادن) meaning “he opened/loosened.” از introduces the thing acted upon; باره تنگ = “the tight (girth/strap)” (here used of the saddle girth). The two verbs share the same subject (he).
segments.s289-g69: نمد زین is a compound noun phrase “the saddle’s felt” (نمد = felt, زین = saddle). به زیر is a prepositional phrase “under/beneath” (به + زیر), and تن خفته مرد is a genitive construction “the body of the sleeping man” (تن = body, خفته = asleep, مرد = man); word order is typical poetic noun‑phrase stacking where modifiers follow the head.
segments.s289-g70: بیفگند is past tense (ب‑ prefix + افکندن) “he threw/laid down.” و coordinates the next verb. نالید is past “he moaned/wailed.” چندی here is an adverb meaning “for a while.” به درد is a short prepositional phrase “with pain/in pain” (به + درد). The sequence describes two successive actions by the same subject.
segments.s289-g71: همه qualifies the following noun (“the whole/entire”). دامن here means the hem or skirt of a garment. قرطه is a small torn garment/patch (a rag), so together دامن قرطه = “the rag’s hem.” را is the definite object marker, and کرد (past of کردن) + چاک (aorist/noun used as result) yields “he made/split (it) — he tore it.” The verb کرد governs the object marked by را.
segments.s289-g72: ابر is used metaphorically as “a cloud” (here of fatigue). خستگیهاش = خستگیها + ـش, “his fatigues/wearinesses.” بر بست is a compound verbal idea: بر (upon/on) + بست (past of بستن) meaning “he bound/closed up upon” (i.e. he constrained or gathered up the cloud). پاک here is adverbial “cleanly/completely.” The phrase means he neatly tied up/checked off his weariness.
segments.s289-g25: برون کرد is a verb phrase 'took out / pulled off' with برون (an adverb/particle meaning 'out') followed by کرد (the verb 'did' used transitively). The actor (رومی, 'the Roman') follows the verb in this poetic word order, then the direct object قبا ('robe'); از introduces a prepositional phrase ('from/out of') attaching to برش. Overall: 'The Roman pulled the robe off (from) ...'.
segments.s289-g26: برهنه شد is an inchoative construction: برهنه ('naked') + شد ('became') = 'became naked'. از marks the source/thing removed (i.e. 'bare of'), ترگ خسته is a compound noun phrase 'worn/tattered rag' (ترگ 'rag' modified by خسته 'worn'), and سرش is 'سر' (head) with the third‑person possessive suffix ش ('his').
segments.s289-g27: ز بس is a poetic causal phrase 'from so much / because of so much'; خون دویدن uses دویدن with a metaphorical sense 'to run/flow' (here 'blood flowing'). تنش is 'تن' (body) + possessive ش 'his', بود is the past 'was', and زرد describes the resulting complexion 'pale/wan'.
segments.s289-g28: دلش is 'دل' (heart) + possessive ش 'his'. پر ز ... is the pattern 'full of ...' (پر 'full' + ز preposition + noun). تیمار here means 'care/anxiety'. The coordinating و links the two parallel phrases, and جان پر ز درد repeats the same construction with جان ('life/soul') and درد as 'pain/sorrow'.
segments.s289-g29: بر آن خستگیهاش begins with the preposition بر ('upon') + demonstrative آن ('that') and خستگیهاش ('his fatigues/fatigue' — خستگی + plural/collective + possessive ش). بنهاد is a past tense verb meaning 'placed / laid (down)' (poetic form of نهادن/بناهاد), and روی here is 'face'—so 'he laid his face upon that exhaustion'.
segments.s289-g30: همی بود is a poetic past/imperfective frame ('he was / he continued to be'); زاری‌کنان is a gerund/participial adverb meaning 'lamenting/weepingly' (describes manner), پیش is the preposition 'before/in the presence of', and اوی is the third‑person pronoun 'him'. The whole segment gives a continuous action: 'he was lamenting before him'.
segments.s289-g31: همی گفت is a frequentative/poetic past 'he said (repeatedly)' with همی adding continuity/emphasis. کای is a vocative particle 'O', followed by the compound adjective نیک‌دل 'kind‑hearted' modifying یار 'friend/beloved', and من is the possessive 'my' (here 'my friend'). Together: 'he said, "O my kind‑hearted friend..."'.
segments.s289-g32: تو رفتی is the simple past address 'you went/you have gone' (تو = 'you', رفتی = past of رفتن). و 'and' links to این بود پیکار من in which این ('this') + بود ('was') + پیکار ('battle/struggle') + من ('my') gives 'this has been my struggle'. The verb بود is past tense linking the statement as a result/conclusion.
segments.s289-g57: This noun phrase lists two objects joined by و (and). سلیح (an older/poetic spelling of سلاح) is the first noun and سر (here ‘head’) is the second; نامبردارشان is a compound-like adjective/pronominal phrase ‘their named/aforementioned one’ modifying سر. Word order is noun + و + noun + modifier, typical in Persian coordination.
segments.s289-g58: ببر is an imperative (‘take!’ or ‘seize!’). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here (‘so that’). بدانند is subjunctive/optative in meaning (‘that they may know’), and پیکارشان is the object of دانستن meaning ‘their fight/battle’. The clause gives the intended result of the imperative.
segments.s289-g59: کنی is the 2nd-person singular subjunctive/imperative form of کردن used here to mean ‘do/make’ in the sense ‘report, inform’. نزد + شاه جهاندار means ‘in the presence of / to the sovereign king’; یاد functions as ‘remembrance/mention’ (i.e. 'tell/mention this'). The whole is an instruction to convey a message to the king.
segments.s289-g60: که introduces the reported content (‘that …’). من is the subject. سر is literal ‘head’ in an idiomatic object phrase; سر به خیره ندادم is a negative past tense clause: ندادم is the past negative ‘I did not give’. به + خیره means ‘to/into astonishment/stupor’ here, and به باد at the end uses باد as the noun ‘wind’ so the idiom means ‘I did not give my head to the wind’ (i.e. I did not throw away my life/honor).
segments.s289-g61: بسودم is a past tense verb ‘I gained / I profited’. به هر جای is a locative phrase ‘in every place/everywhere’. با بخت جنگ is an instrumental phrase ‘by/with the luck (fortune) of war’ — بخت modifies the manner/means (with fortune) and جنگ specifies that fortune as war’s fortune.
segments.s289-g62: گه here means ‘on the occasion / when’ (introducing the next remark). نام جستن is an infinitival/verbal noun construction ‘seeking a name’ (i.e. seeking fame). نمردم is the negative past ‘I did not die’. به ننگ is a prepositional phrase ‘in shame’ (naming the manner of death that did not occur).
segments.s289-g63: به بیژن نمود is a verb phrase with indirect object first: به بیژن ‘to Bijan’ + نمود (past of نمودن) ‘showed’. آنگهی ‘then’ gives the time, and هر دو تور ‘both the two Tūr’ is the direct object shown. Word order (to-PER + verb + adverb + object) is common in older Persian narrative.
segments.s289-g64: که introduces a relative clause modifying هر دو تور: بودند is the past plural of بودن ‘they were’. کشته is a passive/adjectival past participle ‘killed/slain’. فگنده (poetic variant of فکنده/فگنده) functions as a compound participle ‘thrown/scattered’. به دور ‘far away’ gives the location/result. Together: ‘who had been killed and cast far away.’
segments.s289-g41: This is a reporting phrase: 'به بیژن' (to Bijan) places the indirect object first, then 'چنین گفت' ('said thus') is the verb phrase — word order follows Persian SOV in narrative but the verb 'گفت' appears after the adverb 'چنین' (thus). 'کای نیک‌خواه' is a vocative: 'کای' is an older poetic vocative particle 'O', followed by the noun 'نیک‌خواه' (well-wisher, kind-hearted one).
segments.s289-g42: 'مکن' is the negative imperative ('do not'); it attaches before the verb's object here. 'خویشتن' is the reflexive 'yourself' (emphatic object). 'پیش من' is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of me'. 'در تباه' is a locative-compliment meaning 'into ruin' or 'to ruin' (classical/poetic prepositional use). Overall: 'Do not (مکن) [make] yourself (خویشتن) before me (پیش من) into ruin (در تباه).'
segments.s289-g43: The phrase is inverted: 'مرا' (for me/to me) marks the person affected; 'درد تو' is the subject 'your pain/suffering'; 'بتر از' is the comparative construction 'worse than'; and 'مرگ خویش' means 'your own death' (خویش is the reflexive possessive). So the clause reads literally 'To me, your pain is worse than your own death.'
segments.s289-g44: 'بنه' here is an archaic/poetic imperative form meaning 'put/place' (related to نهادن); it commands placement. The doubled 'بر' is a poetic stacking: 'بنه بر سر خسته' = 'place upon the weary head', and the second 'بر' links the next phrase 'ترگ خویش' ('the shred/rag of your own [disposition/garment]'). Grammatically this is a sequence of imperatives and prepositional phrases giving where to place the burden (i.e., put it on your own head/rag).
segments.s289-g45: 'یکی' here means 'some' / 'a certain' (an indefinite 'find something'); 'چاره کن' is an imperative 'make/do a remedy' (i.e., find a way). 'تا' introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that'. 'از این جایگاه' is the source phrase 'from this place/position.' Word order: imperative + purpose marker + prepositional source.
segments.s289-g46: 'توانی' is second-person singular present/potential 'you can' (used with the purpose clause after 'تا'). 'رسانیدنم' is the verbal infinitive/subjunctive 'to bring/deliver me' (the final '-م' marks the object 'me'); Persian often puts the infinitive before the goal phrase. 'نزد شاه' means 'to the king's presence' or 'before the king.' The clause expresses ability to accomplish the act named in the previous segment.
segments.s289-g47: This is an optative/wish construction: 'مرا' ('for me') marks the beneficiary, and 'باد' is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical Persian 'may I have'). 'چندان' means 'so much' or 'to that extent', 'همی' is a poetic continuative/intensifying particle, and 'روزگار' here means 'time' or 'lifespan/fate.' Together they express 'May there be for me so much time...'
segments.s289-g48: 'که' introduces the result/purpose 'that', 'بینم' is the first-person singular subjunctive/potential 'I may see' (from دیدن), 'یکی' here functions as 'once' or 'one (single time)', and 'چهره شهریار' is 'the face (چهره) of the king (شهریار)'. The clause expresses the wished-for outcome: to see the king's face once.
segments.s289-g9: This is a simile phrase: 'چون پلنگان به کام' = 'like leopards in (their) desire'. 'چمان و چران' is a paired nominal phrase meaning grazers/flocks (two related nouns). 'چون' introduces the comparison ('like'). 'به کام' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/at the (beast's) desire' or 'at the mercy of appetite'. Word order is noun phrase → simile → prepositional phrase.
segments.s289-g10: 'نگون گشته' is a perfect/participial construction meaning 'has been overturned' or 'is overthrown' (نگون used as a past/state adjective, گشته as perfect participle/auxiliary). 'زین' = 'saddle' is the affected noun. The coordinating 'و' links the next clause 'گسسته لگام' ('the bridle broken'); 'گسسته' is the past participle 'broken' modifying 'لگام' (bridle). Overall: 'the saddle was overturned and the bridle broken.'
segments.s289-g11: 'همه آلت زین' is a genitive/attributive sequence meaning 'all the implements/gear of the saddle' (آلت = instrument/gear, زین = saddle). 'بر او' = 'upon him' (prepositional), and the second 'بر' with 'نگون' yields 'overturned upon' (literal: 'on him, overturned'). Word order places possessor/possessed together, then the prepositional result phrase.
segments.s289-g12: This is a noun list enumerating what fell: 'رکیب و کمند و جنا'. The three nouns are coordinated by 'و'. 'پر ز خون' is a compound modifier 'full of blood' where 'پر' = 'full (of)' and 'ز' = 'from/of', modifying 'جنا' (corpse). The exact sense of 'رکیب' is context-dependent (rider/companion/rival), here listed as one of the fallen items.
segments.s289-g13: 'چو' here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. 'بیژن بدید آن' = 'Bijan saw that'. The clause 'از او رفت هوش' is a small idiomatic sequence: literally 'from him went awareness' meaning 'his awareness/senses departed' → 'he lost consciousness' or 'he was stunned'. 'رفت' is the simple past of 'to go' used idiomatically to mark loss/cessation.
segments.s289-g14: 'برآورد' here is a classical verb meaning 'brought forth' or 'uttered' (i.e., produced a sound). It governs the following simile 'چو شیر شرزه خروش' — 'a cry like a fierce/raging lion'. 'چو' = 'like', 'شرزه' is an archaic adjective applied to 'شیر' (lion) meaning violent or fierce, and 'خروش' = 'roar/cry'.
segments.s289-g15: 'همی گفت' is a classical imperfective/habitual past idiom meaning 'he kept saying' or 'he was saying'. Here it introduces direct speech. 'کای' is a vocative particle 'O', followed by two epithets 'مهربان نیک‌یار' ('kind, good-friend/noble friend'). Word order is verb phrase then vocative and epithets (typical of poetic address).
segments.s289-g16: 'کجایی' is the interrogative 'where are you?'. 'فگنده' is a past participle meaning 'thrown' or 'cast down' (here 'lying/thrown down'), and 'در این مرغزار' is a prepositional locative phrase 'in this meadow'. Together they form the addressed question: 'Where are you — cast down in this meadow?'.
segments.s289-g1: همه شب بنالید is a past-tense clause: بنالید (3rd-person plural past) 'they wailed/lamented' with the adverbial time phrase همه شب 'the whole night' placed before the verb. تا introduces the temporal endpoint 'until' and governs روز پاک 'the clear/pure day' as the point at which the night-long lamentation ends; پاک is an archaic/poetic adjective meaning 'bright/clear' here.
segments.s289-g2: پر از درد is a predicative/adverbial phrase 'full of pain' (پر + از marking the 'full‑of' relation). چون مار پیچان is a simile 'like a coiling/twisting snake' where پیچان is an adjectival/participial descriptor after مار. به خاک is a directional/result phrase 'onto/into the earth' — the main verb that would normally govern this motion is omitted (poetic ellipsis), so the clause describes the state/result 'to the ground'.
segments.s289-g3: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The clause has the subject گیتی 'the world' followed by the agent/instrumental phrase ز خورشید 'from the sun' and the copulative construction شد + روشنا: شد 'became' and روشنا (poetic form of روشن) 'bright/illuminated' as the predicate adjective. Word order (subject + z‑phrase + شد + predicate) is typical in epic diction.
segments.s289-g4: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. The cluster ب + دان as written here functions as a prepositional/composite element (effectively 'به/بدان' = 'to/into that'), introducing جایگه 'place' as the goal. بیژنا is best read as a proper name (placed after the verb for metrical/poetic effect), so the whole string means 'Bijna came to that place.' Note that دان as given is not used in its usual lexical sense here but as part of the prepositional unit.
segments.s289-g5: همی is a habitual/continuous adverb 'continually/still' placed before the verb. گشت here means 'went/roamed' (past habitual). بر گرد is a prepositional phrase 'around' (بر + گرد), followed by the demonstrative آن and the noun مرغزار 'meadow' as the location being circled.
segments.s289-g6: که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'. یابد is a present/subjunctive form used in narrative to express purpose here ('may/let him find' or 'in order to find'), so its grammatical force is subjunctive rather than simple present. نشانی is the object 'a sign/trace'; ز links that object to the descriptive phrase گم بوده یار, where گم بوده ('had been lost') is a past participial/compound adjectival phrase modifying یار 'companion/beloved'.
segments.s289-g7: پدید آمد is a compound verbal expression 'appeared/manifested' (پدید + آمد). از دور is an adverbial 'from afar'. اسب سمند is a noun phrase in apposition: اسب 'horse' specified by سمند 'steed/warhorse' (سمند is a higher-register, poetic term).
segments.s289-g8: ب + دان again functions as the directional/prepositional element (effectively 'بدان' = 'into/onto that'), taking مرغزار اندرون 'that meadow within' as its goal. چون introduces a simile 'like', and نوند is a rare/technical term or proper name used for comparison (probably the name Navand or an archaic poetic epithet). The clause describes the steed entering the meadow with the likening to نوند.
segments.s289-g49: از آن پس چو مرگ آیدم باک نیست is a temporal clause + comment. از آن پس ('after that') places the time; چو is the classical conjunction 'when'; مرگ (subject) + آیدم is a classical/poetic form meaning 'comes to me' (the enclitic -م marks 'to me'); باک نیست is a negation 'there is no fear'. Word order is topic-time → temporal clause → comment ('no fear').
segments.s289-g50: مرا خود نهالی به جز خاک نیست uses the existential/possessive pattern مرا ... نیست ('for me ... is not', i.e. 'I have no ...'). مرا here is split in the word list (م + را) but functions as the dative/possessive 'for me'. خود is emphatic 'myself'/'own'; نهالی is the possessed noun 'a sapling/young shoot'; به جز is the idiom 'except for'; خاک is 'earth/soil'; the final نیست negates existence. The whole clause says the speaker has nothing but earth.
segments.s289-g51: نمرده است هرکس که با کام خویش begins with نمرده است, a negated past-participle construction (ن + مرده + است) meaning 'is not dead'. هرکس که introduces a relative/conditional 'whoever/anyone who'. با + کام خویش means 'with his own desire' (کام = wish/desire; خوی + ش is the possessive 'his own'). The clause sets up the condition for the following verb.
segments.s289-g52: بمیرد بیابد سرانجام خویش contains two verbs in sequence: بمیرد (classical/subjunctive or narrative present form of 'to die') expresses the action 'dies' under the condition; بیابد is a present/subjunctive form of یافتن 'to find/attain' meaning 'he attains/finds'; سرانجام خویش = 'his outcome/fate' (سرانجام = outcome/final result; خویش = his own). The construction is conditional: 'whoever dies with his desire fulfilled, attains his fate.'
segments.s289-g53: و دیگر دو بد خواه با ترس و باک lists people: و دیگر دو بدخواه = 'and two other ill-wishers' (بدخواه is a compound meaning 'one who wishes ill'); با ترس و باک is a doubled prepositional phrase 'with fear and with dread' adding descriptive circumstance. Word order: connective → determiner/adjective → noun → descriptive phrase.
segments.s289-g54: که بر دست من کرد یزدان هلاک is a relative clause introduced by که 'who/whom/that'. بر دست من کرد literally 'made (it) by my hand' or 'did at my hand' — دست here can mean 'power/hand' ('at my hand' = by my agency). یزدان هلاک is the agent/causer phrase 'God (made) [them] perish' where هلاک is a noun/adjective 'destroyed/perished'. The classical word order places the causative/doer phrase after the verb.
segments.s289-g55: مگرشان به زین بر توانی کشید begins with مگرشان (مگر + شان) where مگر functions as a conditional 'unless' here. به زین بر = 'onto the saddle' (به + زین + بر emphasizes movement onto); توانی is the present/subjunctive second-person 'you can' (توانی = you are able); کشید is a past/infinitival root in classical usage meaning 'to pull/bring/drag' (here infinitive-like following توانی). The clause expresses a conditional capacity: 'unless you can pull them onto the saddle.'
segments.s289-g56: وگرنه سرانشان ز تن‌ها برید contrasts with وگرنه 'otherwise'. سرانشان is literally 'their heads/leaders' (here physical heads); ز تن‌ها 'from the bodies' (ز = 'from'); برید is a past form meaning 'were cut off' / 'were severed' (classical short past). The clause states the violent consequence if the prior condition fails.
segments.s289-g81: ز فتراک بگشاد = the verb phrase: ز (ablative 'from/off') + فتراک (saddle‑cloth) + بگشاد (past of گشاد with the prefixed b‑, here 'opened/loosened'). The sequence means 'he loosened/offed (it) from the saddle‑cloth.' زان پس کمند is a short adverbial continuation: زان پس 'thereupon/then' introduces that next action and کمند (lasso) is the object to be used in the following clause.
segments.s289-g82: The clause z ترکان یکی را به گردن فگند is the main action: ز ترکان یکی = 'one of the Turks' (poetic, partitive use of ز + plural noun + یکی), را marks that phrase as the definite object, به گردن = 'around/onto the neck' (به as directional/placement), and فگند is the past verb 'threw/cast'. Together: 'he cast it round the neck of one of the Turks.'
segments.s289-g83: ز اسب اندر آورد = 'he took (him) from/off the horse': ز (from/off) + اسب (horse) + اندر آورد (compound verb 'brought in/removed' here meaning 'dismounted or removed from'). The object (the Turk) is omitted but understood. و زنهار داد = 'and he gave a warning' where زنهار (imperative root used as noun) = 'a warning' and داد = 'gave'.
segments.s289-g84: بدان کار با خویشتن یار داد breaks down as بد(=به)+دان+کار = 'بدان کار' functioning as 'regarding that affair' or 'to that matter' (به + demonstrative/old form) and با خویشتن یار داد = 'he gave/made (him) a companion with himself' i.e. 'he made him his ally/companion.' Note the object 'him' is again implied rather than repeated; داد is transitive 'gave/made.'
segments.s289-g85: وز آنجا بیامد = 'and/from there he came' — وز is a poetic conjunctional/locative (here effectively 'and from') and بیامد is the past 'he came.' به کردار گرد = 'in a circling manner' or 'in the manner of going about': به (in/with/regarding) + کردار (manner/deed) + گرد ('around' / indicating circling). The phrase describes his mode of approach.
segments.s289-g86: دمان سوی لهاک و فرشیدورد = a directional phrase: دمان (skirt, hem, edge — here the edge/skirts of the place) + سوی (toward) + لهاک and فرشیدورد (following names/terms). Grammatically سوی governs a following noun phrase; the sequence means 'toward the skirts/edge, to Lahāk and Farshidvard.'
segments.s289-g87: بدید آن سران سپه را نگون = 'he saw those leaders of the army overthrown.' بدید is simple past 'he saw'; آن سران سپه (that/those + plural 'leaders' + 'army') is the definite object marked by را; نگون is an adjectival/predicative describing their state ('overthrown / downfallen').
segments.s289-g88: فگنده بر آن خاک غرقه به خون = participial/locative detail: فگنده (thrown/laid) attaches to the previously mentioned leaders; بر آن خاک = 'upon that earth/soil' (بر = on/upon, آن خاک = that soil); غرقه به خون = 'drenched in blood' where غرقه is 'drenched/immersed' and به + خون expresses the material/agent (lit. 'with/in blood'). Word order places the participle and the location/description after the noun it qualifies.
segments.s289-g33: This clause expresses a present obligation. شتابم is a first‑person form (verb with enclitic -م) meaning “I hurry / I must hurry”; کنون = “now”. بیش = “more” modifies the hurry. بایست کرد is a periphrastic modal: بایست = “must/ought” and کرد (past 3sg) functions as the auxiliary, so the whole string says that an increased haste is necessary now: “I must hurry more now.”
segments.s289-g34: An infinitival/purpose phrase: رسیدن is the infinitive “to reach/arrive (at)”. بر before the pronoun تو marks the target/directedness toward you (i.e. “to/at you”), and به جای نبرد gives the destination “to the place of battle” (به = to, جای = place, نبرد = battle). The whole phrase gives the purpose/result of the haste: “to reach you at the battlefield.”
segments.s289-g35: مگر introduces a contrary/counterfactual rhetorical clause (≈ “were it not / unless”). بودمی is the archaic past 1sg form “I was / I had been” (older word order uses -می ending). گاه سختیت = “the time/occasion of your hardship” (سختیت = hardship/difficulty with the possessive -ت “your”). یار is a vocative/address (“O friend/companion”) placed after the phrase.
segments.s289-g36: چو is the temporal conjunction “when”. با + اهرمن = “with Ahriman” (i.e. in conflict with the demon). ساختی is 2nd person past of ساختن “you made/you did” and here idiomatically governs کارزار (“waged/ made war”) — so the clause means “when you waged battle with Ahriman.” The subject “you” is omitted because it is encoded in ساختی.
segments.s289-g37: کنون = “now.” کام دشمن = “the enemy’s desire/wish” (کام + genitive). همه راست کرد is an idiomatic past construction: همه = “entirely/all,” راست کرد = “made true / fulfilled.” کرد is the past transitive ‘made/caused,’ so the clause means the enemy’s will was completely realized or their desires were fully satisfied.
segments.s289-g38: برآورد is past 3sg of a verb meaning “brought about / accomplished.” The remainder of the clause, سر هرچ می‌خواست کرد, continues with reported action: سر here is literal “head” but appears as part of the action cluster, and هرچ می‌خواست کرد = “(he/they) did whatever (he/they) wanted,” where می‌خواست is the imperfect/past habitual ‘desired/wished’ and کرد is the main past “did.” Overall: “(they) brought about / carried out whatever they wished.”
segments.s289-g39: بگفت is the narrative past “(he) said.” Objects and subjects are postposed in classical order here: این سخن is the object “this speech/these words,” and بیژن immediately after it is the subject (“Bijan said this”). The conjunction و begins the next clause and گستهم is the proper name Gostaham (a person), so گستهم starts the following action; note that گستهم here is a name, not the listed verbal sense.
segments.s289-g40: بجنبید is past/narrative intransitive “he stirred / was roused / moved.” و links to the next verb. بر as a prepositional/verb prefix with زد gives the sense “struck against / struck at”; زد is the past of زدن “struck.” یکی = “one / a single,” تیز = “sharp / swift,” and دم here (poetically) denotes the tip/edge or a single swift stroke — so the clause describes Gostaham’s movement and a single sharp strike.
segments.s289-g73: This is a prepositional + directional phrase: وز آن جایگه = “from that place” (وز is a poetic/archaic variant of 'از' meaning ‘from’). سوی بالا = “toward up/ upwards,” and دوان is an active participle/adverbial (running/rushing) placed after the direction; the whole string functions as the initial adverbial clause describing the motion before the main verb.
segments.s289-g74: بیامد is a simple past “(he) came.” The phrase ز غم تیره کرده روان is an instrumental/ablative + participial phrase: ز غم = “from/because of grief,” تیره کرده is a perfect/participial construction “having made dark,” and روان (here) = “spirit/mind.” Grammatically this whole phrase modifies or complements the subject of بیامد: “he came, his spirit made dark by grief” (i.e. he arrived with his spirit darkened by sorrow).
segments.s289-g75: Noun phrase + predicate + verb: سواران ترکان = “the horsemen of the Turks” (possessed construction: possessed noun followed by possessor), پراگنده is a predicate/adjective “scattered,” and دید is the past-tense main verb “(he) saw.” Word order is noun + modifier + verb, with the subject omitted as typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s289-g76: که introduces a relative clause modifying سواران. آمد … پدید is a two-part verbal phrase meaning “came/appeared”; specifically پدید آمد (appear/come into view) is split by the prepositional phrase ز راه بیابان = “from / out of the desert road.” So the relative clause reads “who came/appeared from the desert road.”
segments.s289-g77: A compact adverbial simile and motion clause: ز بالا = “from above,” چو برق = “like lightning” (چو introduces the simile), اندر آمد = “came in/ descended” (اندر is an archaic/prepositional particle meaning ‘in/into’ placed before the verb), and به شیب = “to/onto the slope.” The whole segment describes rapid downward motion using poetic inversions common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s289-g78: دل is the affected noun “the heart.” از + noun (از مردن) here expresses the source/ground of emotion—“from/by the thought of dying” (i.e. with dread of death). گستهم is an older/poetic perfective/participial form bound to the subject (its exact nuance is archaic; here it functions adjectivally/verbally to indicate a resultant state or action upon the heart). با نهیب is an instrumental phrase “with a cry/shout” (نهیب = a loud/thunderous cry). Together: “he (or his action) left the heart, by the dread of death, in such a state, with a cry” — idiomatically, “with a cry he struck the heart with dread of death.”
segments.s289-g79: از آن بیم is an ablative/causal phrase “because of that fear.” دیده here is the noun “eye” and in context forms a compact nominal cluster with سواران (دیدهِ سواران) — i.e. the perception of the horsemen — but in poetry such nominal clusters can be elliptical; دو تن = “two persons/two bodies” is the numeral + classifier that names the victims. Grammatically the phrase sets up who is affected or seen: “because of that fear, (the eyes of) the horsemen (saw) two persons” — more idiomatically, “two of the horsemen (were).”
segments.s289-g80: به شمشیر = “by/with the sword” (instrumental). کم کرد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: کم کردن here means “to make few / to reduce the number of” — in a battle context it idiomatically means “to cut down/slay.” زان انجمن = “from that assembly/host” (زان = from that; انجمن = assembly/band). Together the clause states the action: “(he) cut down two with the sword from that band” (i.e. two were killed).
segments.s289-g17: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the complaint clause. پشتم is the noun 'back' with the 1st‑person possessive suffix (-م) 'my' (written here as one token). شکستی is a 2nd‑person past verb 'you broke / you have broken'. و simply coordinates the two verbs. خستی here is another 2nd‑person past verb meaning 'you wearied (made tired)' and دلم (= دل + -م) is the object 'my heart' (دل = heart, -م = my). The whole segment therefore reads 'that you broke my back and wearied my heart.'
segments.s289-g18: کنون is the adverb 'now' starting the next clause. جان شیرین is a collocation 'sweet life' (جان = life/soul, شیرین = sweet). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. تن is 'body', the source from which the life will be torn. بگسلم is a 1st‑person optative/subjunctive form of گسستن with the verbal prefix ب‑ (here meaning 'may I / I will tear away'); syntactically the verb comes at the end as typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s289-g19: بشد is a past/perfect form of شدن used here in the sense 'he mounted / he got on'. بر پی اسب is a fixed phrase meaning 'upon the back of the horse' (بر = on; پی = on/after/back), and بر چشمه‌سار gives the direction 'toward the spring/streamlet'. Word order places the verb before the series of prepositional phrases, as is common in epic narration.
segments.s289-g20: مر is an archaic/datived pronoun form meaning 'me / to me' used here idiomatically as a short object/experiencer marker; او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks the object). بدید is the past 'saw'. اندر آن مرغزار is the locative phrase 'in that meadow' (اندر = in; آن = that; مرغزار = meadow). The overall sense is narrative: 'he saw him in that meadow.'
segments.s289-g21: همه begins the noun list: 'all / every'. جوشن is 'cuirass, armor' and و links to ترگ 'rags' (a contrast of full armor and torn clothing). پر here is the postpositive adjective 'full (of)'; it combines with خاک و خون 'dust and blood' to mean 'full of dust and blood'. This is a compact descriptive nominal phrase common in epic lines.
segments.s289-g22: فتاده is the past participle 'fallen' (he has fallen). بدان is the fused sequence meaning 'to/into that' or 'by that' (ب = to/into; دان here functions as a demonstrative/compound element 'that' in older usage), linking the following خستگی 'weariness' as the cause. سرنگون is an adjective 'overthrown / cast down'. Together: 'fallen, by that weariness, overthrown.'
segments.s289-g23: فروجست is a strong past of فروجستن 'to leap down / to spring down', here 'he leaped down'. بیژن is the subject name. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. شبرنگ is used as the mount's name or descriptive epithet (here functioning as the thing he leaped from — the horse), and زود is the adverb 'quickly'. The verb precedes the subject for narrative force.
segments.s289-g24: گرفتش is the verb گرفت + accusative clitic -ش meaning 'he seized him / he took him'. به آغوش is the prepositional phrase 'into (his) embrace' (به = into/to; آغوش = embrace/bosom). در تنگ is an idiomatic descriptor 'tight / close' (در = in; تنگ = close/tight), so 'in a close embrace'. The final زود repeats the adverb 'quickly' for emphasis. The object clitic shows who is being seized; verb comes before the phrase.
segments.s289-g97: This phrase functions as a prepositional/time phrase meaning 'from there toward the Arabs.' وز is an archaic fused form corresponding to به/از (i.e. 'from/and from'), آنجا = 'there', سوی = 'toward', and تازیان = 'the Arabs' (plural). The word order is typical classical Persian: directional preposition + place + target.
segments.s289-g98: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came.' به سان is a fixed simile marker 'like/as,' followed by the noun phrase پلنگ ژیان 'a (living) leopard' (ژيان here qualifying پلنگ). The simile modifies the manner of coming.
segments.s289-g99: فرود آمد is a compound meaning 'he alighted / descended' (past). از اسب is the source phrase 'from the horse.' و links the clauses; او را is the explicit object marker + pronoun 'him' (the person being handled); چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' that sets the manner for the following verb.
segments.s289-g100: This is a cluster of manner and result: بی‌آزار نرم are adverbial/adjectival modifiers 'harmlessly, gently' placed before the verbal phrase. از بر زین is a prepositional phrase 'from off the saddle' (از = from, بر = on/upon, زین = saddle). نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed / set down' and takes the earlier او را as its object.
segments.s289-g101: بدان ترک is an archaic phrase meaning 'to that Turk' (بدان = به آن). فرمود is past 'he said/commanded.' تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / telling him to...') and is followed by برنشست 'sat up on / mounted' (past form used with purpose sense).
segments.s289-g102: به آغوش او is a dative/goal phrase 'into his embrace' (به = to, آغوش = embrace, او = his). اندر is an archaic preposition 'into/within' reinforcing direction. آورد is past 'brought' and دست 'hand' is the direct object; together the clause means he put his hand (around him) into his embrace — idiomatically 'took him into his arms.' Word order places the goal phrase before the verb, typical of Persian.
segments.s289-g103: سمند نوندش is 'his steed Navand' (نوندِ + -ش possessive). همی راند is a classical/imperfective flavor meaning 'kept driving/urged on' (همی adding continuous aspect), and نرم modifies the verb as manner 'gently.' The clause describes how the horse was ridden.
segments.s289-g104: بر او is 'toward him' (to him). بر همی is an idiomatic/poetic repetition where بر + همی intensifies or marks recurrent action; آفرین is the acclamation 'bravo' or 'well done' and خواند is 'he called/sang' (here 'called out'). گرم modifies the verb as manner/adverbially 'warmly/enthusiastically.' Word order places the indirect object phrase before the verb, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s289-g89: This phrase sets the location and patient: به + سرشان (“to/on their heads/them” with the clitic -شان) marks what will be placed; بر اسبان جنگی is a prepositional phrase “upon/on war-horses” and جنگی modifies اسبان as “warlike/war (i.e. war-) horses.” The short phrase به پای (‘to/at the foot of’) narrows the place to “at the horses’ feet” and anticipates the following verb (‘make’) that completes the action.
segments.s289-g90: سازید is a 3rd-person plural/polite-subjunctive form of ساختن used here as a performative imperative/optative meaning “make” or “cause to be.” چراگاه سازید = “make (it) a pasture”; the following و جای چرای repeats and specifies the idea (جای = place, چرای here is an archaic/poetic verbal noun/infinitive ‘grazing’), a tautological pair typical in epic diction for emphasis.
segments.s289-g91: چو is the temporal conjunction “when”; بیژن is the subject (proper name); چنان modifies the seeing (“thus/so”); دید کرد is the literary compound (perfective) with دید “saw” + auxiliary کرد, common in epic Persian to narrate a single completed act; آفرین is an interjection meaning “Bravo!/well done!” (here exclaimed by Bijan).
segments.s289-g92: ابر گستهم here functions as a vocative/metaphorical phrase “O cloud (i.e. mighty one) Gostaham” — گستهم is a proper name (not the literal sense given in the lemma list). کو is an interrogative/adverb “whence/where” in a poetic register; سرآورد is a past-tense verb meaning “has brought forth/produced,” and کین is the noun “vengeance/hatred,” so the clause asks from what source this wrath/vengeance has arisen.
segments.s289-g93: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning “he commanded/said”; تا introduces purpose or result (“so that/that”); ترک is the noun “Turk” (here referring to the Turkish fighters); زنهار is an admonition “beware!/take heed” used as the content of the warning, and خواه (here modal) expresses desire/command (“let him/wish that he”) — together تا ... زنهار خواه = “that the Turk be warned/take heed.”
segments.s289-g94: This is the concrete command: به زین برکشید is motion toward the saddle (به + زین = “to/onto the saddle”) + برکشید “raise/lift” (past/subjunctive/imperative sense in reported command). آن سران را uses آن + سران with the object marker را to mark “those chiefs” as the direct object, and ز راه is a prepositional phrase “from/off the road” (ز = from). The full clause thus orders that those chiefs be taken up from the saddle and removed off the road.
segments.s289-g95: ببستندشان is a past plural transitive verb with the 3rd-person plural object clitic -شان: “they tied/bound them.” The coordinated nouns دست و پای (“hands and feet”) are the direct objects of that action; the following و میان (“and between/among the middle”) links into the manner in which they were handled and leads into the next clause.
segments.s289-g96: کشیدند is a past plural verb “they dragged/drew”; بر پشت زین کیان is the prepositional phrase indicating where—بر = “on/upon,” پشت = “back,” زین = “saddle,” and کیان = “the Kayanian (kings/the royal house)” used as a genitive modifier of زین. The object is omitted here because it is the same pronominal object bound in the previous segment (them).

sentences.s289-s53: Could he possibly bring him alive to the king from the battlefield?
sentences.s289-s54: He urged on Bijan, Bijan full of pain and sorrow; his spirit full of grief, I wandered.
sentences.s289-s33: He said this, and a weakness seized his spirit; Bijan could no longer hold up his head.
sentences.s289-s34: From that place he at once brought his horse and loosened the tight girth.
sentences.s289-s35: He threw the saddle’s felt beneath the sleeping man’s body and moaned in pain for a while.
sentences.s289-s36: He tore the whole hem of the rag and cleanly bound up the cloud of his weariness.
sentences.s289-s13: The Roman pulled the robe off him; his head became bare of the worn rag.
sentences.s289-s14: So much blood had run that his body was pale; his heart was full of care and his life full of pain.
sentences.s289-s15: He pressed his face upon that exhaustion and remained weeping before him.
sentences.s289-s16: He kept saying, 'O kind‑hearted friend of mine, you have gone, and this has been my struggle.'
sentences.s289-s29: Seize their weapons and the head of their named man, so that they may learn about their fighting.
sentences.s289-s30: Report (it) to the sovereign king, that I did not give my head to the wind.
sentences.s289-s31: I profited everywhere by the fortune of war; when I sought a name, I did not die in shame.
sentences.s289-s32: Then he showed Bijan the two Tūr (men), who had been killed and thrown far away.
sentences.s289-s21: He said to Bijan thus: “O well-wisher, do not bring yourself to ruin before me.”
sentences.s289-s22: Your suffering on my account is, for me, worse than your own death; put it upon your weary head, upon the shred of your own disposition.
sentences.s289-s23: Find some remedy so that you may be able to bring me from this place to the king.
sentences.s289-s24: May I have so much time that I may see the king's face once.
sentences.s289-s5: Like leopards, the grazers and their flocks were at the beasts' will; the saddle was overturned and the bridle broken.
sentences.s289-s6: All the saddle's gear fell upon him: the companion (rider), the lasso, and a corpse all covered with blood.
sentences.s289-s7: When Bijan saw that, his senses left him; he raised a cry like a raging lion.
sentences.s289-s8: He kept saying, 'O kind, noble friend, where are you, thrown down in this meadow?'
sentences.s289-s1: They wailed all night until the clear day; full of pain, like a coiling snake, (they) to the ground.
sentences.s289-s2: When the world grew bright from the sun, Bijna came to that place.
sentences.s289-s3: He kept going around that meadow so that he might find a sign of the lost companion.
sentences.s289-s4: From afar a steed appeared; into that meadow it entered, like Navand.
sentences.s289-s25: After that, when death comes to me I have no fear; I myself have no young shoot except the earth.
sentences.s289-s26: No one is dead who, having died with his desire fulfilled, attains his own end.
sentences.s289-s27: And two other ill-wishers, trembling with fear and dread, whom God destroyed at my hand.
sentences.s289-s28: Unless you can draw them onto the saddle, otherwise their heads will be severed from their bodies.
sentences.s289-s41: He loosened the saddle-cloth; then he seized a lasso and cast it round the neck of one of the Turks.
sentences.s289-s42: He pulled him off the horse and gave a warning; he made him his ally in that affair.
sentences.s289-s43: From there he came, moving in a circling manner toward the skirts/edge — toward Lahāk and Farshidvard.
sentences.s289-s44: He saw those leaders of the host overthrown, thrown upon that earth, drenched in blood.
sentences.s289-s17: Now I must hurry more to reach you at the place of battle.
sentences.s289-s18: Had I not been present, O friend, at the time of your hardship, when you made war with Ahriman?
sentences.s289-s19: Now the enemy’s desire was wholly fulfilled; they carried out whatever they wished.
sentences.s289-s20: Bijan spoke these words, and Gostaham was roused and struck a single sharp blow.
sentences.s289-s37: From that place he ran upward; he came with his spirit darkened by grief.
sentences.s289-s38: He saw the Turkish horsemen scattered, who had appeared coming from the desert road.
sentences.s289-s39: From above, like lightning he came down the slope; with a cry he shook the heart with the dread of death.
sentences.s289-s40: Because of that fear, two of the horsemen of that band were cut down with the sword.
sentences.s289-s9: That you have broken my back and wearied my heart — now let me tear my sweet life from my body.
sentences.s289-s10: He mounted onto his horse and rode to the spring; he saw him in that meadow.
sentences.s289-s11: All his cuirass and rags were full of dust and blood; fallen from that weariness, he lay overthrown.
sentences.s289-s12: Bijan leaped down from Shabrang quickly; he seized him and clasped him tightly in his embrace, quickly.
sentences.s289-s49: From there he came toward the midst of the Arabs like a living leopard.
sentences.s289-s50: He dismounted from his horse and, like the wind, gently and harmlessly set him down from the saddle.
sentences.s289-s51: He told that Turk to mount, and he took him into his embrace.
sentences.s289-s52: He urged his steed Navand gently onward; warmly he cried out 'bravo' to him.
sentences.s289-s45: Place their heads at the war-horses' feet, and make them a pasture and a place for grazing.
sentences.s289-s46: When Bijan saw that, he cried 'Bravo!' — 'O cloud of Gostaham, whence has this wrath arisen?'
sentences.s289-s47: He ordered (it): 'Let the Turk take heed; lift those chiefs from the saddle off the road.'
sentences.s289-s48: They bound their hands and feet and dragged them along between, upon the backs of the Kayanian saddles.
